## [00:00 - 03:01]

15 years ago, I had a powerful realization that completely changed the trajectory of my career. Your career will never outgrow your communication. And after a decade of speaking and training communication skills to millions of people, I've discovered there are four levels of speaking that you need to move through if you want to grow faster in your career, business or relationships. Level one, unaware. This is where you have no idea what you're doing wrong. You think you're saying the right things, but you're repelling people without even realizing it. Level two, aware. You understand the skills now required to be a great communicator, and now you know what you're doing wrong. Level three, skilled. Here you're practicing. You have the tools, but it takes deliberate focus and intention. You feel like you're performing the skill and it doesn't feel like you're embodying it. Level four, mastery. At this level, communication is second nature to you. You just think about the outcome and your communication skills take you to where you want to go automatically. And understanding which level you're stuck on is crucial because at each and every level, there's one core skill you need to learn in order to be able to advance to the next stage. So let's start with level one. The problem here is you don't know what you don't know, which is why if you want to move to level two of knowing you need education. Just by watching this video, subscribing to my channel and attending one of my communication classes, you'll be learning the fundamental skills that make you great as a communicator. To save you time, let me share exactly what you need to know to be a great communicator. Number one, this is your vocal image. And that's how you use your voice. It's your volume, your rate of speech, your pitch, your tonality, and your use of pauses. If you look at the greatest speakers in the world, they don't get stuck in a default volume, default pitch, default rate of speech. No, no, no, no. They move from loud to then quiet. They go from slow to then fast. They go from high pitch to then low, filled with authority. You get the point. Great communicators are dynamic with how they speak. Number two, this is visual image. This is how you use your body. And the greatest communicators are hyper aware of what they're communicating non-verbally. Your body language consists of your hand gestures, eye contact, facial expressions, and the way you use movement as you're talking. And number three, verbal image. This is how you use your words. For example, when you communicate, do you use storytelling to help get your points across? Or do you share just raw data, facts, and information? Do you use frameworks when you communicate? Or do you just ramble and share your thinking out loud? Once you understand these three areas, vocal image, visual image, and verbal image, you never look at communication the same way again.

## [06:05 - 09:10]

turn up the volume and turn the video face down so you can only hear yourself. Start taking notes on your vocal image. Am I speaking too fast? Am I pausing enough? Am I too quiet? What's distracting about my voice? Start to take notes as you isolate the vocal image. Second, now we're doing the visual review. Turn down the volume. Turn the video face up so you can only see yourself, but you can't hear anything. Take notes on your visual image. Do I sway my body around while I'm talking? Do I vary my facial expressions? Am I stuck in what they call an RBF? You can Google that later what that means, resting blank face. Am I darting my eyes? What's distracting about my body language? What are the non-functional behaviors here that I see? Start taking notes. And finally, the verbal review. Here, get your video transcribed so you can see exactly what words you used, and then start to highlight the words you use all the time that add no value and sometimes distract people from the answers and the clarity of your message.
